name: Thomas Crawley Knowles   Year One
summary: Thomas Crawley Knowles arrived in Washington, D.C., on July 30, 2016 ‚Äì his great-grandmother Molly McMillan‚Äôs 97th birthday.

photos.title: Thomas Crawley Knowles
photos.caption: Newborn

photos.title: Thomas Crawley Knowles
photos.caption: October 2016

photos.title: Thomas Crawley Knowles
photos.caption: November 2016
December 2016

photos.title: Thomas Crawley Knowles
photos.caption: January 2017

photos.title: Thomas Crawley Knowles
photos.caption: Not too sure about pears ...

photos.title: Thomas
photos.caption: February 2017, with Opa and Oma

photos.title: Thomas Crawley Knowles
photos.caption: March 2017

photos.title: Chunks? Really?
photos.caption: Thomas was not pleased to encounter a banana in chunk form.

photos.title: Crawlster
photos.caption: Thomas started to crawl in early May 2017.

photos.title: Up before dawn and ready to roll
photos.caption: His parents, not so much

photos.title: Mimi’s Strawberry Cream Roll
photos.caption: Thomas had a special early first birthday cake in Portsmouth: His great-grandmother’s famous strawberry cream roll. (His mother remembered it from her own fourth birthday.) Thomas liked it enough to attempt a face plant.

name: Dick McMillan
summary: Dick McMillan was a flight instructor during World War II, certified for carrier landings, founder of his own electric motor company, churchman, choir member, avid hunter and fisherman.

photos.caption: Dick McMillan, left, with his older brother Malcolm.

photos.title: Father and Sons
photos.caption: Malcolm, left, and Dick with their father in July 1929. Childhood polio, Dick said, had left their father with thin legs but a powerful upper body.
Dick McMillan in the summer of his 14th birthday
Dick McMillan in his late teens
Molly and Dick, Marnie and Mal
Dick McMillan, U.S. Navy flight instructor, ca. 1940, with his red convertible.

photos.title: Traveling
photos.caption: On vacation with the Napiers
Dick and Molly met in the choir at House of Hope. Years later, Dick was still singing; Molly was ordained.

photos.title: Father of the bride
photos.caption: Dick McMillan at the wedding of his daughter Allison and Mark Nickel, July 26, 1986, at the House of Hope.

name: Molly McMillan
summary: Mary Allison (Molly, Nana) McMillan lived a long and eventful life: Vassar physics major, civic leader, one of the earliest Twin Cities women ordained in the Presbyterian church, great-grandmother to 10.

photos.title: Bigelows
photos.caption: Mary Allison Bigelow (Nana) with her parents C.H Bigelow and Allison McKibbin Bigelow.

photos.title: Molly and Grammie
photos.caption: Molly McMillan with her step-grandmother Mary Henderson Dorsey McKibbin, known as “Grammie,” ca. 1920.
Molly McMillan, about 5, taken in 1924.
Molly McMillan, taken July 1, 1924 a month before her her fifth birthday.
Molly, likely in her college years, taken at Manitou Island. (“The Hollow” is in the background.)
Molly McMillan in the summer of 1942.

photos.title: Molly McMillan
photos.caption: Wife, mother, and community leader.

photos.title: Traveling
photos.caption: On vacation with the Napiers
Dick and Molly met in the choir at House of Hope. Years later, Dick was still singing; Molly was ordained.

photos.title: The Three Allisons
photos.caption: Mary Allison McMillan, Allison Butler, and Mary Allison (Molly) McMillan met up at the wedding of Kerry McMillan and Chris Stemland.

name: Dick and Molly‚Äôs wedding
summary: Dick and Molly were married in the Bigelow family home. A quick honeymoon during the war years amounted to a short fishing trip ‚Äî her “first and last,” as Molly later wrote.

photos.title: Father of the bride
photos.caption: Molly’s father, Charles H. Bigelow, walks his daughter down the stairs to begin the wedding ceremony at the Bigelow family residence.

photos.title: Departure
photos.caption: With the ceremony and reception behind them and Molly changed for travel, the couple heads down the stairs and out to the waiting car.

photos.title: Honeymooners
photos.caption: Dick and Molly leave the wedding reception in Dick’s convertible.

name: Adelheid Mueller
summary: Adelheid was the fourth of Ernst and Helen Mueller‚Äôs nine children and was thought to be the most delicate, the least likely to survive childhood. She died at age 92, with four children, eight grandchildren, and one great-grandchild.

photos.title: ca. 1910
photos.caption: Possibly the first photo taken of Adelheid. Writing on the back says she was 3 years old, eating a piece of butter bread with Pete the turkey in Madison, Minnesota.
The childhood photo is a detail from a snapshot of Adelheid with her two younger brothers, Ernst and Kurt. Adelheid was six and a half, according to Opa’s handwritten note on the back.
Adelheid holds her younger brother Max at Max’s baptism. Max died of a common childhood disease before his seventh birthday. Adelheid would have been nine years old for the photo.

photos.title: Off to college
photos.caption: Adelheid, violin in one hand and hugging kid sister Norma with the other, prepares to leave for Dr. Martin Luther College in New Ulm, Minnesota, about 50 miles northeast of Freedom Township. Her brothers Ernie (with toothache) and Kurt were driving her.

photos.title: Another call
photos.caption: Adelheid’s first call was to Lacrosse. This call to Baraboo, dated April 4, 1931, was sent to Lacrosse, but Adelheid had already received a call to St. Lucas in Milwaukee in the fall of 1930.

photos.title: College grad
photos.caption: Adelheid would go from her teaching job in La Crosse to a school at St. Lucas Church in Milwaukee.

photos.title: First job
photos.caption: Adelheid’s first teaching job was in La Crosse, Wisconsin, just as the Great Depression was starting.

photos.title: The wedding party
photos.caption: With the bride and groom, from left: Gottfried Schultz (very likely), and sisters Frieda, maid of honor, and Norma, flower girl.

photos.title: First-born son
photos.caption: Adelheid and Joel in the yard at 553 North Marion Street in Oak Park, Illinois, where the young family had settled in a second-floor flat.

photos.title: Summer of ’55
photos.caption: Jim, 11 at the time, asked to take a picture of his mother. Aunt Frieda gave him her box camera and some film and Jim shot scenes in Eagle Lake, Minnesota. Mimi was 48.

photos.title: Au revoir
photos.caption: Mimi’s retirement farewell at Zion, Bensenville, with Mark, Dean Lueking, Nonny, Jim, and Al Gehlhausen.

photos.title: Farewell to 623
photos.caption: With the house at 623 North Ridgeland empty and her belongings already in a moving van, Mark, Mimi, and Pooch posed for a final snapshot and then set out for Portland, Oregon, in Mimi’s red Buick Skylark.

photos.title: Heading West
photos.caption: Mark drove Mimi and Pooch from Chicago to new retirement digs at Ione Plaza in Portland. The route naturally included a stretch of Highway 14 through Eagle Lake, Minnesota.

photos.title: Still planting
photos.caption: Adelheid was gardening as a kid in Freedom Township, Minnesota, and continued well into her 80s in Oregon – here at Joel’s house in Stayton.

name: Hilbert C. Nickel
summary: H.C. (Hib) Nickel was the first American-born Nickel, first college graduate (Marquette University), and first to earn a graduate degree (M.B.A., University of Chicago). He was a stock broker, market analyst, business teacher (Indiana University‚ÄìGary), church treasurer, and father of four boys.

photos.title: On South Howell
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel, maybe two or three years old, did not seem particularly pleased to sit for a family portrait.

photos.title: Confirmand
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel was confirmed at St. Lucas Lutheran Church, probably 1919 or 1920.

photos.title: High school
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel’s high school graduation picture.

photos.title: College grad
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel, graduating from Marquette, was the first Nickel to attend college.

photos.title: The wedding party
photos.caption: With the bride and groom, from left: Gottfried Schultz (very likely), and sisters Frieda, maid of honor, and Norma, flower girl.

photos.title: Family man
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel and his first two sons, Jimmy and Jerry (James and Joel), outside the family’s second-floor flat on Marion Street in Oak Park.

photos.title: During the war
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel, with supervisers, served as an accountant at the American Shipyard.
One of H.C. Nickel’s clients gave son Mark a Kodak Brownie Starflash camera when Mark was hospitalized with osteomyelitis. This would have been ca. 1956.
H.C. Nickel in the early 1960s.

name: The Four Nickel Boys
summary: All born and raised in Oak Park, Illinois, but no longer in the Midwest. Joel and Tim are in Oregon; Jim in New York City, Mark in Rhode Island.

photos.title: Hot Rod
photos.caption: Building a hot rod — recruiting materials, finding tools — could take a day or more. Design evolved as the work progressed (note the trendy convertible roof). Probably summer of 1950 or ’51 in Eagle Lake, Minnesota.

photos.title: Eagle Lake
photos.caption: The three-railed Lionel ranked well below the American Flyer and even farther below the HO gauge, but it would have to do for the summer. This could have been the summer of 1950, a few months before Tim’s arrival.

photos.title: Penny pictures
photos.caption: A trip to the photographer in Mankato was part of the August summer ritual.

photos.title: The dugout
photos.caption: The older Nickel boys and their summer friends in Eagle Lake, Minnesota, built a baseball diamond in an empty field behind the C&NW station. Tim had the famous little Cosmo glove; Mark had a three-fingered antique possibly from Grampa Nickel’s team in the 1920s; Jim had his beloved Reach.

photos.title: Woods South of Bagley
photos.caption: There is no record of Uncle Boo-Boo owning this particular model, but he did drive several old jalopies for their final mile, abandoning them “in the woods south of Bagley [Minnesota].”

photos.title: Say cheese!
photos.caption: Someone always had “the buck” on an outing. Forced by circumstances to smile, Mark — *Bockhans* for the day — reached for exaggerated cheerful effect and unwittingly produced the famous “neck cords” shot. The four brothers returned to Sibley Park in June 2007 but could not find the spot.

photos.title: Postcard-perfect Icon
photos.caption: No week at Melahn’s Cabins on Lake George, Minnesota, was complete without a trip to Bemidji and a visit to Paul Bunyan and Babe the Blue Ox, a massive 1937 work of concrete and steel. Tim found the postcard in a flea market and sent it to Mark as a 70th birthday present in 2018.

photos.title: In Minnesota
photos.caption: Likely one of the last Eagle Lake photo sessions in Mankato. Once Joel started staying in Oak Park for a summer job, the long visits to Eagle Lake came to an end.

photos.title: Found art
photos.caption: The two Aces visited Rhode Island in August of 1984, according to a note on the back. They created a sculpture out of found junk at the Haffenreffer grant in Bristol. They later found the Jimmy and Jerry, a lobster boat in Newport harbor.

photos.title: Nickel Boys
photos.caption: All four gathered at Joel’s house in Stayton for a wedding, but whose?

photos.title: Memory lane
photos.caption: The four brothers did a Midwest tour in June 2007, Hib and Addie’s 100th birthday. Milwaukee, where they found the grave of Michael Nickel and Wilhelmina Knoll, was the final stop before wrapping up in Chicago.

photos.title: In Brooklyn
photos.caption: In the courtyard of the Green Building at Anson and Reva’s wedding, June 20, 2015.

name: Ernie and Charlotte Mueller
summary: In a town largely without television or telephones, Ernie and Charlotte had bonfires, marshmallow roasts, and sessions of “strict rules” Flinch. Charlotte taught; Ernie kept a large garden ... and was an early adopter of the Edsel.

photos.title: Ernie Mueller, age 2
photos.caption: Ernst, the fifth of the Mueller children and the last born in Madison, Minnesota, turned 2 on June 27, 1911. Little more than two months later, the family moved to Freedom Township.
Opa had sent Ernie to Coyne Electrical School in Chicago, the extent of Ernie’s formal education. Ernie started an electric motor repair service, but it never got off the ground.

photos.title: Minnesota Muellers
photos.caption: In July 1986, Adelheid visited Herb, Kurt and Ernie, her three surviving brothers, at Ernie’s house in Eagle Lake. She was in Minnesota for Mark and Alli’s wedding in St. Paul.

name: Allison McKibbin
summary: Allison McKibbin, mother to Mary Allison (Molly) Bigelow and her younger brother John, was the second wife of C.H. Bigelow Jr.

photos.caption: Allison McKibbin, 2 years and 3 months, taken December 1883
Allison McKibbin, left, with her step-sister Anne held by nurse Jennie Chandler, dated 1888.
Allison McKibbin, February 1893
Allison McKibbin Bigelow
Allison McKibbin Bigelow, Molly McMillan’s Mother
Allison McKibbin, taken August 26, 1911, on her wedding to Charles H. Bigelow Jr.

photos.title: Bigelows
photos.caption: Mary Allison Bigelow (Nana) with her parents C.H Bigelow and Allison McKibbin Bigelow.

name: Joel Thomas Nickel
summary: Joel ‚Äî he was Jerry through high school ‚Äî was the pathfinder: athlete, student leader, creative minister in challenging inner-city Detroit and south-side Chicago, father, grandfather, artist.

photos.title: First-born son
photos.caption: Adelheid and Joel in the yard at 553 North Marion Street in Oak Park, Illinois, where the young family had settled in a second-floor flat.

photos.title: Sisters and cousins
photos.caption: Erna Marquardt and Adelheid Nickel introduced cousins Irma and Joel, probably in late summer 1939 at Box 43, the Marquardt farm outside Janesville, Minnesota.

photos.title: Four generations
photos.caption: Four generations: Joel Nickel, held by his father Hilbert Nickel, next to his grandmother Margaret Ziesemer Nickel next to his great-grandmother Ulricka Zäske Ziesemer. Taken in the backyard of the Nickel family home on Howell Avenue very likely by Joel’s mother Adelheid Mueller Nickel, possibly in summer of 1940.

photos.title: Early cousins
photos.caption: **Early cousins:** The first four of Oma and Opa Mueller᾿s 17 grandchildren were Irma and Enno Marquardt (top and middle left) and Joel and James Nickel (middle and bottom right). The remaining two children are not identified but could be Marquardt cousins, possible Phil or Len.
Joel and Jim in Lake Elysian, outside Janesville, Minnesota, ca. summer of 1947. This shot led to the family term “Storckbein” — stork legs.

photos.title: Easter
photos.caption: Jimmy and Jerry Nickel with their Easter egg haul.

photos.title: Confirmation
photos.caption: From left: H.C., Joel and Adelheid Nickel; grandmothers Margaret Nickel and Helen (Oma) Mueller; aunts Frieda and Erna Mueller with Erna’s husband Ernst Marquardt; brothers Mark and Jim with, possibly, cousin Beth Grams.

name: Thomas Crawley Knowles   Volume II
summary: About six weeks after his first birthday, Thomas and his parents left Washington, D.C., for 4021 West Grace Street in Richmond, Virginia. Corner lot, fenced back yard, community with lots of kids and young families.

photos.title: New kid on the block
photos.caption: Thomas and his parents arrived in Richmond, Virginia, in September 2017. His new front door is on the corner of Grace Street and Westwood Avenue.

photos.title: Explorer
photos.caption: Thomas found a special hiding place near the back door in the kitchen of his new house.

photos.title: Blue skies
photos.caption: Thomas has a public playground for little kids — no dogs allowed — about a block away from his new home. The cast protects his left wrist, injured when he misjudged the distance from a bed to his intended landing spot.

photos.title: Halloween
photos.caption: Thomas visited a pumpkin patch, an ideal place for his growing vocabulary. He ran from one pumpkin to the next shouting “Ball! Ball! Ball!’

photos.title: Sprinter
photos.caption: Thomas, in Portsmouth for an overnight visit, enjoyed his expanding locomotive talents in the wide open spaces of Oma and Opa’s backyard.

photos.title: Tree hugger
photos.caption: Thomas went hunting for his first Richmond Christmas tree.

photos.title: Al fresco
photos.caption: Thomas on the Grace Street terrace, eating a bowl of penne with Opa.

photos.title: Ring Bearer II
photos.caption: Like 3-year-old Opa, 2-year-old Thomas was a ring bearer — at Jessica Fye’s wedding on August 11, 2018. By all accounts, Thomas did a little better. On August 10, 1952, little Opa stopped at the third pew from the narthex. No one could persuade him to continue.

photos.title: Layout
photos.caption: On Thanksgiving weekend in 2018, Oma, Opa, and Thomas’s whole family went to a model railroad show at the Science Museum in Richmond. Thomas preferred the O gauge.

photos.title: Big machines
photos.caption: Lots of interest and excitement arrived with the crew that dug up Thomas’s street and connected the water main to the “Bang-Bang House.” That’s what Thomas called the new house being built next door with its months of incessant hammering.

photos.title: Empire Builder
photos.caption: Thomas enjoyed his first layout, combining an Amtrak-ish passenger train with Thomas the Tank Engine. Even the inevitable derailments were fun: “Oh no! Train broke.”

photos.title: Saturday in the Park with Oma
photos.caption: Oma and Opa were in town when baby brother James Mark arrived. Thomas took them on several visits to his park.

photos.title: Big brother
photos.caption: Three weeks after his little brother’s birth, Thomas was settling into his new role.

photos.title: Snow? In Richmond?
photos.caption: A huge rogue snowstorm hit much of the South, including Richmond. Thomas did his part to help cope.

photos.title: Wonder years
photos.caption: After Oma and Opa got on the plane back to Rhode Island, Thomas went to the Science Museum with his mom and James. He found the turtle on the second floor.

photos.title: Every dog’s dream
photos.caption: Dry dog food makes perfect play dirt for earth-moving toys. Smokes didn’t object.

photos.title: Explorer
photos.caption: An afternoon visit to the Richmond Science Museum is one of Thomas’s favorite things to do. There’s so much for an inquiring mind to discover — lots of it specially designed for kids.

photos.title: Ready to roll
photos.caption: At breakfast, a Richmond city work crew began grading the alleys and spreading gravel in Thomas’s neighborhood. They parked the steamroller for Sunday and Thomas climbed aboard.

photos.title: Bunny ears
photos.caption: Thomas’s school introduced him to the Easter egg hunt on the Thursday before Easter 2019.

photos.title: In the paddock
photos.caption: Thomas was waiting for his turn on horseback at the Waldorf School fair. He’ll start at Waldorf in the fall.

name: Anson Edouard Nickel
summary: The youngest kid in the household learned to stand his ground. If he was watching *Charlotte‚Äôs Web* and Dan wanted to watch a Bruins game, Anson would put his foot down: “I watchin‚Äô *pig!*”

photos.title: Zonked
photos.caption: No half-measures for Anson. When he was active, he was *very* active and when he crashed, he was deeply asleep.

photos.title: Brio master
photos.caption: Anson was a little more than two when he started working with Brios. This was around Christmas 1989.

photos.title: Architect
photos.caption: Anson inherited some Duplos from older siblings and acquired a few of his own. Legos were a short way off. Designing structures and spaces became a lifelong thing.

photos.title: No soap
photos.caption: Anson, not quite three in the summer of 1990, attended a soap show in Boston with Uncle Binyon and Aunt Ting. He wasn’t too sure about a bar of soap containing chocolate.

photos.title: Big diggies
photos.caption: Anson’s term for earth-moving equipment. He brought his front loader to Second Beach in the fall of 1990.

photos.title: The Dude on his ranch
photos.caption: Anson and his cousins and his uncles and his aunts were at Vista Verde in Colorado to celebrate his grandparents’ golden anniversary.

photos.title: OK Corral
photos.caption: Anson was sitting on the corral fence at Vista Verde, waiting for the “Tenderfoot Rodeo” to begin, part of Dick and Molly’s golden anniversary celebration in 1993.

photos.title: Coyotes even
photos.caption: In the spring of 1995, Mom and the three kids visited Nana and Grampa in Arizona. (Dad had to be in Washington to run a press conference.) Anson, 8 years old, filled him in on the visit.

photos.title: Toothless grin
photos.caption: Hockey had nothing to do with it.

photos.title: Ice Man
photos.caption: Everyone agreed: Anson got his hockey skills from his mother, who also helped coach the mites.

photos.title: Test case
photos.caption: Anson spent a few days and nights as a research subject in Mary Carskadon’s Butler Hospital sleep lab, all wired up to record brain activity. A grad student TA shot the Polaroid.

photos.title: Steamboat 1998
photos.caption: Nimble and lightweight, 11-year-old Anson plunged fearlessly down the double-black-diamond trails. His somewhat larger, self-taught father stuck to the greens and a few of the blues.

photos.title: Eighth grade
photos.caption: Top of the heap at Pennfield, but about to be a high school freshman. Anson had his choice of St. Georges and Tabor Academy. He headed for Tabor. Is that the slightest hint of a wise-guy smirk?

name: Daniel McMillan Crawley
summary: Oldest kid, pathfinder, and entrepreneur, Dan‚Äôs first business was buying bags of Jolly Ranchers and selling them by the piece on the school bus. It was successful but short-lived. Portsmouth public schools eventually shut him down.

photos.title: Newborn
photos.caption: Peter, proud father, holds Daniel January 30, 1982, two days after Dan’s birth.

photos.title: Spitting image
photos.caption: Dan, at seven months, would grow up to look a lot like Peter.

photos.title: Up and running
photos.caption: Dan in his first summer on two feet, probably June or July 1983.

photos.title: Little Engine
photos.caption: Dan, not yet 2 but already into books, had just become a big brother.
Dan, three months before his third birthday, was a large green frog for Halloween. His sister and brother would have their turn.

photos.title: Grandma Esther
photos.caption: Dan with his paternal grandmother, probably fall of 1985 .

photos.title: Christmastime 1985
photos.caption: The dump trucks and Tonka vehicles are still new, probably Christmas 1985.

photos.title: New Family, Day One
photos.caption: Dan, 4, with dog and blanket outside House of Hope after his parents’ wedding July 26, 1986.

photos.title: How does it do that?
photos.caption: Dan, 4, was on a visit to Oregon in June 1986, flying a kite on the beach. He turned out to be pretty good at it.

photos.title: Still flying
photos.caption: Plenty of sun and wind on the Oregon coast kept Dan’s kite way up.

photos.title: Now We Are Six
photos.caption: The football field birthday cake was Dan’s idea. Definitely homemade, definitely chocolate. Dan's birthday is within range of the Super Bowl.
Alli took Dan, Susan, and Anson to Sedona, Arizona, to visit Dick and Molly. Mark had to say behind and run a press conference in Washington.

photos.title: A good walk spoiled
photos.caption: Do we hike Sachuest or golf? Aliki and Alli hiked through nine holes, then headed for the clubhouse. Dan and Mark played all 18 holes, finishing about an hour before dusk. Boxing Day 2018.

photos.title: August 27, 2021
photos.caption: Dan and Aliki were married on the terraza of Aliki’s house in Agios Lavrentios.

name: Susan Allison Crawley
summary: Susan, the middle kid sandwiched between brothers, picked up a noticeable Fall River accent (pizzer, youse, bwanners), developed a strong preference for tuna-pea casserole and was fearless about large creative projects like painting an entire wall of the basement.

photos.caption: Susan, not quite 2, at the beach with Alli.

photos.title: Basketful
photos.caption: Susan, a bit before her second birthday, in the Tiverton house near Lana‘s.

photos.title: Reading with Nana
photos.caption: Likely around Christmastime, 1985.

photos.title: New home
photos.caption: The house at 101 Gideon Lawton Lane was still new to everyone, including Susan, not quite 4.
Susan, 5, during a trip to Busch Gardens in Florida, where Nana and Grampa had a condo at Innisbrook.
Susan, 5, on a warm October day in 1988.

photos.title: Big sister
photos.caption: Anson had arrived not quite a year earlier.

photos.title: A girl and her dog
photos.caption: Zoe, the original chocolate lab saw Susan through middle and high school.

name: Archer Gaur Nickel   Year One
summary: Archer Gaur Nickel arrived in Brooklyn on a beautiful spring Wednesday, May 2, 2018.

photos.title: Newborn
photos.caption: Archer Gaur Nickel arrived on May 2, 2018, at about 12:19 p.m.

photos.title: The First Family
photos.caption: Archie, all 6 pounds, 8 ounces of him, met Mom and Dad on Wednesday, May 2, 2018.

photos.title: At home in Brooklyn
photos.caption: Archie on his first full day in his apartment across from Prospect Park.

photos.title: Buckled up and ready to ride
photos.caption: At the end of his first week, Archie was headed for an outing. Car or stroller, when he’s in his seat he’s ready for either.

photos.title: Day 21: Brooklyn
photos.caption: On his three-week birthday, Archie summoned his grandparents — Opa, Oma, Nana, and Nani — to his home in Brooklyn. They were thrilled.

photos.title: Eyes that miss nothing
photos.caption: Oma and Opa had some wonderful time with Archie while his parents took a walk in Prospect Park. He was happy, expressive, and attentive to everything around him.

photos.title: Mother Nature’s son
photos.caption: Happy guy Archie and his mom were on an outing to Prospect Park with Nani – Sunanda – who took the snapshot.
Anson and Archie, 13 June 2018

photos.title: Introductions
photos.caption: Archie, one day after turning four months, locks eyes with Uncle Dan, who was in Portsmouth with Aliki for Labor Day.

photos.title: Eyes of the tiger
photos.caption: For his first Halloween, Archie showed up in a tiger suit that will probably fit him for a couple years.

photos.title: Jackson Pollack
photos.caption: Archie’s first encounter with purple sweet potato purée was colorful and drippy, but he enjoyed the solid food experience.

photos.title: Office 2.0
photos.caption: Archie has long enjoyed his “office,” a colorful area with things to look at, grab, and play with. He moved into a new, bigger office around Thanksgiving 2018: More variety, more complexity, more fun.

photos.title: First haircut
photos.caption: It was Dad’s first effort too, and it turned out quite well.

photos.title: Colorful guy
photos.caption: Archie, with four teeth and a renegade red ball, a few days before his first birthday.

photos.title: Milestone
photos.caption: Archie’s first birthday — Uno de Archie — was celebrated in festive Cinco de Mayo style. (“Speedy Gonzales” was standing, thinking about walking ...)

photos.title: All A-Taut-O
photos.caption: Archie showed up at daycare sporting a bib from his father’s alma mater.

photos.title: Motoring
photos.caption: Archie, in Rhode Island for his father's 10th (and Oma’s 45th) reunion at Brown, mostly mastered the uneven backyard terrain at Oma and Opa’s house.

photos.title: Critic
photos.caption: Great Uncle Binyon’s *Venture Piece* had a magnetic effect on little eyes. Archie was transfixed when he discovered it in Uncle Dan’s living room in Madrid. He pointed at it, stared at it, tried to figure out what it was.

photos.title: Corn dog
photos.caption: There‘s only one proper way to eat corn on the cob and it doesn‘t require tools.

photos.title: Cousins
photos.caption: Archie studies photographs of cousins Thomas and James. The three little cousins were together for the first time over Christmas 2019 in Richmond.

name: James Mark Knowles   Volume I
summary: James Mark Knowles arrived in Richmond with the autumnal equinox just before midnight on Sunday, September 22, 2018.

photos.title: Newborn
photos.caption: James Mark Knowles arrived in Richmond with the autumnal equinox just before midnight on Saturday, September 22, 2018, checking in at 9 pounds 11 ounces.

photos.title: Serene and stylish
photos.caption: At one week, James was sporting a double-breasted onesie.

photos.title: Bright eyes
photos.caption: James at about six weeks.

photos.title: Rolling over and proud of it
photos.caption: Figuring out those complex motor skills has to start somewhere. James added rolling over to his skill set a few days before Great Uncle Joel’s 80th birthday. Now on to sitting up!

photos.title: Strongman
photos.caption: Having mastered rolling over, James is enthusiastic about tummy time.

photos.title: Working on his core
photos.caption: Little James surprised everyone by “planking” while trying to figure out how to crawl. At six months, he seemed eager to move — and was very proud of himself.

photos.title: A special little place
photos.caption: Like his big brother, James enjoys the little park a block away from his house. The swings just fit.

photos.title: Up with the chickens
photos.caption: James, on vacation in mid-July 2019, proved to be an early and happy riser.

photos.title: Happy guy
photos.caption: James is a strong, active guy. He crawls, walks, and climbs constantly, particularly *up* the stairs.

photos.title: It’s me!
photos.caption: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, what do you see? I see Jamesy in a basket looking at me.

photos.title: Brushes optional
photos.caption: When a new shipment of art supplies arrived on Grace Street, James tried out the tempera paints.

photos.title: *Sommerhitze*
photos.caption: In July 2020, Richmond and Covid-19 offered outdoors types little refuge from the heat. The backyard offered no shade after 9:30 a.m., so James and his family headed back inside.

photos.title: At the wheel
photos.caption: At the neighborhood tot lot, James took the wheel of a favorite primary-colored two-door coupe and loved the experience.

photos.title: Speedster
photos.caption: Learning to walk was *so last year.* Scooters get you there faster — with a helmet and good brake for new riders who can't help but push the limit.

photos.title: Dining with Dino
photos.caption: Dinosaurs of all kinds and sizes were a big hit when they arrived from Great-uncle Binyon and Great-aunt Ting. The triceratops prefers banana slices.

photos.title: Hmmm. How does this work?
photos.caption: Unfamiliar stuff, this snow. Rare in Richmond, it may have been James’s first encounter with a Minnesota-style winter.

photos.title: Red Car
photos.caption: James and Oma spent a long time examining and playing with the tiny Red Car. Like Great Uncle Tim with “Bearsie,” James could not sleep without it and was very sad. In the morning after a night when no one could find it, James’s Mom drove to Target and bought four of them.

name: Galapagos Islands 2018
summary: Trite but true: It was the trip of a lifetime, including an arrival at the same spot in Dagus Cove where Charles Darwin came ashore in 1835.

photos.title: A patch of green
photos.caption: A collapsed lava tube developed into small pond where flamingos could feed on brine shrimp. The site and a few others like it provided a welcome visual contrast to an otherwise bleak island landscape dominated by *pehoihoi* lava fields.

name: Oma and Opa Mueller
summary: Helen Hedder and Ernst Mueller were both children of immigrant parents. They began married life in Madison, Minnesota, a stark contrast to their lives in late 19th-century German Chicago.

photos.title: Bridesmaids
photos.caption: A handwritten note on the reverse says “Mother [Helen Mueller] and Great Aunt Emmie [her aunt Emily Luecke], bridesmaids at Great Uncle George Luecke’s wedding in August in St. Louis.”

photos.title: Young love
photos.caption: Ernst Mueller (Opa) and Helen Hedder (Oma) courted in and around Chicago. There were picnics at Concordia Cemetery in Forest Park and, above, outings in what could be Humboldt Park, half a block from Oma’s mother’s house. They were married April 12, 1899.

photos.title: Newlyweds
photos.caption: Oma and Opa – Helen Hedder and Ernst Mueller – were married April 12, 1899, in Chicago. She was 21; he was 24.

photos.title: *Aller Anfang ist schwer*
photos.caption: Ernst Mueller’s first call was to a southern Minnesota dual parish — Zion in Madison (Arena Township) and St. John’s Lutheran Church in Madison. He was ordained at Zion on August 28, 1898, returning to Chicago in April 1899 to marry Helen Hedder.

photos.title: In Madison
photos.caption: Oma and Opa had five kids in Madison and would have four more in Freedom Township. The five, clockwise from left: Adelheid, 2; Erna, 9; Ernst, maybe 10 weeks; Frieda, 5 1/2, and Gerhard 4 1/2. Photo probably dates from fall 1909, two years before the move to Freedom Township.

photos.title: Arena Township
photos.caption: Opa’s main congregation was St. John’s in Madison, Minnesota, but he also served Zion, a rural church in Arena Township. The five children, clockwise from left: Erna, Adelheid, Gerhard, Frieda, Ernst.

photos.title: Waldorf
photos.caption: When he took the call to Freedom Township, Opa served the Waldorf congregation as well. This is the new church, dedicated December 24, 1911, about three months after he arrived. It was replaced in 1940.

photos.title: Now we are six
photos.caption: Opa and Oma’s first six kids: Erna, top; Frieda and Gerhard below her; Adelheid and Ernst below them; and Kurt, who was the first to be born in Freedom Township. Still to come: Herbert, Max, and Norma.

photos.title: Tin Lizzie
photos.caption: Opa and Oma were among the earliest automobile owners in Freedom Township, Minnesota.

A family portrait probably taken late in 1924 or early 1925. The sons, from left: Ernst; Kurt, Herbert (seated) and Gerhard. The daughters, from left: Adelheid (casually showing off the watch she received for high school graduation), Erna, Norma and Frieda.

photos.title: Good Thunder
photos.caption: **Good Thunder:** The three ministers in the front row, from left, are Paul Mueller, Ernst (Opa) Mueller, and Heinrich (Hai) Fiertag, Opa’s brother-in-law from Colorado. The man directly behind Opa and the woman with the child on her lap are the Pautsches, with whom the three men stayed during a pastoral conference in Good Thunder, Minn. At far right is Teacher Wegner from Freedom. The Pautsches’ daughter Lorraine would become Opa’ daughter-in-law; the child is Lorraine’s brother Arvid.

photos.title: Father and sons
photos.caption: From left: Ernie, handyman and gardener; Gerhard, LCMS minister; Kurt, pharmacist and WW II veteran;and Herbert, LCMS minister.

photos.title: Mother and daughters
photos.caption: Paul Grams, one of Oma’s grandsons, once remarked that the women were the strong one in the Mueller family. Erna and Frieda are standing behind Norma, Oma, and Adelheid.

photos.title: With Oma and Opa
photos.caption: Jerry (Joel) and Jimmy visited Oma and Opa at Freedom township, probably in the mid-1940s.

name: Mark and Alli
summary: We started out as a foursome and reached the full complement in October 1987.

photos.title: Party of Four
photos.caption: Danny and Susie were part of the wedding ceremony, each receiving an engraved small silver plate. Uncle Tim, the best man, had a pocket full of gummi bears just in case.

photos.title: Engaged
photos.caption: Mark, Alli, Dan, and Susan visited Portland in June 1986 to meet the West Coast family.

photos.title: First Family Photo
photos.caption: Anson was born in the car October 24, 1987, at 2:40 a.m. about a mile short of the hospital. Mark drove home the next morning, switched cars, picked up Danny and Susie at Lana’s house, and returned to Women & Infants Hospital. (Lana arrived at our house shortly before 2 a.m., then took the kids to her house for breakfast in the morning.) Mark adopted Danny, 5, and Susie, 3, in January 1987.

photos.title: At Singers
photos.caption: The Providence Singers held a baby shower for Christine Noel, who was pregnant with Sophia, ca. fall 2015.

photos.title: Horsey set
photos.caption: Alli managed to secure two places at Newport Polo’s fundraiser. About two dozen Providence Singers were on hand for for a Sunday afternoon wine, cheese, picnic supper, and polo match July 2017.

photos.title: Tierra Opera
photos.caption: We had lunched at the Thiessen Gallery, but we stopped in at Dan’s newest Tierra, across from the Royal Opera. It’s a beautiful, inviting place – and not quite two weeks old.

photos.title: Old Folks and the Sea
photos.caption: The email caption read: “I fear the Pandemic Beard, already at full Hemingway, will reach full Johannes Brahms before we have a vaccine.”

name: Thomas and James
summary: The two guys, born just over two years apart, are growing up together in Richmond.

photos.title: Big brother
photos.caption: Three weeks after his little brother’s birth, Thomas was settling into his new role.

photos.title: Brothers
photos.caption: 8 March 2019

photos.title: At play
photos.caption: Thomas and James were in their backyard horsing around in mid-October 2019. That’s the Bang-Bang House in the background, constructed on an empty lot shortly after Thomas moved in.

photos.title: Riding the rails
photos.caption: Thomas and James explored a bit of railroad history at the Science Museum, which used to be Richmond’s train station. The Science Museum is a favorite place.

photos.title: At home in Richmond
photos.caption: With James fully mobile and increasingly communicative, the brothers are finding lots of things to do together. Pulling on boots and jumping into puddles after a rainstorm is a speciality.

photos.title: Springtime
photos.caption: The winter of 2020 wasn't much of a winter in Richmond. February was warm enough to horse around outside in light jackets.

photos.title: Diversion
photos.caption: A set of paints was a welcome change after two months of the stay-at-home Covid-19 experience. The brothers plunged right in. James was a little more free-form.

photos.title: Straightaways
photos.caption: With plenty of track and plenty of room to build, the brothers built a layout with a minimum of turns and a maximum of long hauls.

photos.title: Hmmmm
photos.caption: James ponders the situation while his big brother prepares to restore a wheel that has fallen off the tractor.

photos.title: The eat-in kitchen
photos.caption: Thomas and James love their cornbread and prefer the kitchen to the dining room for their breakfast. With neither table nor chairs, seating is where you find it.

photos.title: *Après le déluge: Nous*
photos.caption: Thomas and James found puddles everywhere after a whole morning of rain in January 2021. Puddles, of course, are perfect for punschering.

photos.title: Great packing
photos.caption: Snow arrived in Richmond Sunday, January 31, 2021. Heavy, wet, ideal for snowballs and snowman-making — and a perfect way to celebrate Dad’s birthday.

photos.title: Campfire, March 2021
photos.caption: On the last night of Opa and Oma’s visit to Richmond, there was a grand fire in the fire pit and hamburgers for supper. A beautiful night.

name: Frieda Mueller
summary: Flett, Fifi, Lili ‚Äî all nieces and nephews had a special family name for her ‚Äî was the most beloved of aunts. She was caregiver for her aged parents, counselor for bachelor brothers, family historian, a cohesive and positive force across four generations.

photos.caption: Frieda, right, and her younger sister Adelheid at a “popcorn party” their mother organized for kids at the church school.

photos.title: Collegian
photos.caption: Like some of her siblings, Frieda attended DMLC — Doctor Martin Luther College — in New Ulm.

photos.title: Mother and daughters
photos.caption: Paul Grams, one of Oma’s grandsons, once remarked that the women were the strong one in the Mueller family. Erna and Frieda are standing behind Norma, Oma, and Adelheid.

A family portrait probably taken late in 1924 or early 1925. The sons, from left: Ernst; Kurt, Herbert (seated) and Gerhard. The daughters, from left: Adelheid (casually showing off the watch she received for high school graduation), Erna, Norma and Frieda.

photos.title: Frieda Mueller, L.P.N.
photos.caption: Frieda moved from St. Paul to Arlington Heights, Illinois, in the late 1960s, where she began working at the Lutheran Home for the Aged. She earned a nursing license and served as a senior charge nurse until she retired and became a resident.

name: Archer Gaur Nickel   Volume II
summary: With walking under his belt, Archie started his second year with a new goal: Words. He knows what to call buses.

photos.title: Bookworm
photos.caption: Lots of interesting stuff on farms — tractors especially.

photos.title: *Blockflöte* guy
photos.caption: On a weekend getaway with his parents and their friends, Archie found a house with lots of things for kids, including a soprano recorder. Opa was pleased.

photos.title: Maestro
photos.caption: Archie found lots of fun things to do with Nani and Bob while his parents were in California for a week.

photos.title: Beach Boy
photos.caption: With most New Yorkers keeping their COVID-19 social distance at home, Archie had acres of uninhabited sand to dig in and explore.

photos.title: Shades
photos.caption: Sheltering in place during the pandemic gets old very fast. Archie was happy to ride in the car maybe to a deserted beach, maybe to Bob and Nani’s house.

photos.title: Apple harvest
photos.caption: Archie and his parents visited a farm, looked at the pigs, goats and chickens, and then set to the serious work of apple picking. They came home with 25 pounds.

photos.title: Worthy of his hire
photos.caption: An outing for apple-picking isn’t only about picking apples. Archie and his parents sampled the apple-infused goodness of just-fried apple doughnuts

photos.title: Scholar
photos.caption: Archie loves his school, Covid precautions and all.

photos.title: How do you say *Spätzle*?
photos.caption: Archie, lover of all things pasta, enjoyed his first encounter with the famous German noodle, part of a holiday care package from Binyon and Ting.

photos.title: The Three Guys
photos.caption: Archie was fascinated with washers, dryers, and the whole laundry process. Tom was amazed at the changing words and numbers on the display. James was just happy to be there.

photos.title: Maltmaker
photos.caption: His dad actually made it, but Archie was on hand to help deliver chocolate malts to the cousins at Oma and Opa’s house.

photos.title: Atop Hanging Rock
photos.caption: Archie, Anson, and Opa were the first visitors of the day at the Norman Bird Sanctuary. Three large deer bounded across the trail. There were turtles on the rocks, lots of bird sounds, and many trails.

photos.title: Cherrypicker
photos.caption: During a four-grandchild visit to Oma and Opa’s house, Archie helped bring in the 2021 sour cherry harvest, much of which Opa turned into jam. Squeezing out the pits was the best part.

photos.title: Young scholar
photos.caption: First day of preschool, 2021 — a very happy time.

photos.title: Diwali guy
photos.caption: Doing his part to celebrate the victory of light over darkness, Archie enjoyed his first experience with live fireworks.

photos.title: Winter Storm Kenan
photos.caption: The snowblower did the heavy lifting but there was still lots left to do.

name: Immigrants
summary: There were 11 M√ºllers aboard *Der Adler* ‚Äì Fritz, Johanne, and nine kids ‚Äì all traveling first cabin.

photos.title: Georg Wilhelm Mueller
photos.caption: A charcoal sketch, possibly created by his son Fritz Mueller, appeared on a Family Search website in 2018. A note on the back says “by FGW Mueller” and has the date as 1865. It may have been handed down by Gertrude Mueller Rehwinkel.

photos.title: Early in 1847
photos.caption: Fritz, Johanne, and their first three children, from left, Elizabeth, Theodora, and Wilhelm. Wilhelm looks to be about six or eight months, so the photo would likely have been taken in Dorum in the spring of 1847. Emigration was nearly two decades away.

photos.title: *Familie Müller* arrives at Castle Garden
photos.caption: The 11-member Mueller family arrived in New York harbor on Sunday, October 1, according to Fritz’s journal, and went ashore at Castle Garden the next day. They traveled in first cabin and were the first people listed on Capt. B. Forstmann’s 362-passenger manifest given under oath to Port of New York authorities. All the Muellers, even the small children, were listed as German farmers headed for Indiana: Fritz Müller, male, 42 (note umlaut in U.S. records); Johanna, female, 45; Theodora, female, 21; Lisabeth, female, 20; Wilhelm, male, 18; Erich, male, 16; Emma, female, 15; Friederich, male, 10; Enno, male [mistakenly entered as female], 9; Hans, male, 6; and Gertrude, female, 5. The listed ages likely reflect the point at which Fritz booked passage, since, Fritz, Wilhelm, Erich, Friederich, Hans, and Gertrude were actually a year older on arrival than their recorded ages.

After he sold Bosenbüttel, Fritz Mueller moved most of his money to America through Bremen banks. He kept some of it in gold coins, which he put into this money belt that Johanne wore on the emigration to Indiana.

photos.title: Johanne Rösing
photos.caption: Johanne would have been 45 years old when she landed in Indiana with husband Fritz Mueller and their nine children. She wore the money belt with gold coins. The undated photograph belonged to her great-grandson Frederick Miller, last relative to own the Mueller farm in Kendallville.

photos.title: Erich E.A. Mueller
photos.caption: Erich, a self-described Christian socialist, lost his career in pecan polishing to automation late in the 19th century. His political writings figured into a defamation lawsuit by a Lutheran minister against his congregation.

photos.title: In St. Louis
photos.caption: Erich E.A. Mueller and Maria Carolina Bergt, seated, with their nine children. Rear, from left: Gustav, Martha, Adolf, Theodora. Front, from left: Gerhard, Emma, Louisa (in white), Magdalene, Elizabeth.

photos.title: Indiana wedding
photos.caption: Friedrich C.J. Mueller and Mary Mertz worked the Mueller farm east of Kendallville and secured clear title to the land.

photos.title: Heir
photos.caption: This photo appears to have been taken about the time of Friedrich’s marriage to Mary Mertz.

photos.title: Heir
photos.caption: Friedrich C.J. Mueller, Fritz and Johanne’s sixth child, owned and lived on the family farm after his older brother Wilhelm left farming and moved to Chicago.

photos.title: Johannes and Fredericke
photos.caption: First row from left: Olga, Theodor, Clara, Paul. Top from left: Edwin, Hilde, Lydia, Johannes, Julia, Frederick.

name: Gerhard Mueller
summary: Gerhard, an ordained LCMS minister, was the most enigmatic of Ernst and Helen‚Äôs nine children. He served small parishes in Nebraska and tiny missions in northern Minnesota, subsisting below the poverty line. Siblings called him Gene or P√§sel. Neices and nephews knew him as Boo-Boo. No one really knew why.

photos.title: Athlete
photos.caption: Gerhard finished his high school career in Waldorf, where he was valedictorian and a member of the varsity basketball team, such as it was.

photos.title: Seminary graduate
photos.caption: Gerhard graduated from Concordia Seminary, Springfield, in the class of 1930. His friends remembered “Pongo” at the 35th reunion, which Gerhard attended.

photos.title: Nebraska
photos.caption: Gerhard behind the wheel of his Packard in pre-Dust Bowl Nebraska.

photos.title: Way to hustle, babe!
photos.caption: Gerhard’s winter diversion was the Bemidji high basketball team. When they made it to the state tournament, the kids on the team chipped in to get Gerhard a bus ticket. The story by Jim Klobuchar (Sen. Amy Klobuchar's father) was on Page One of the Saturday Minneapolis Morning Tribune, March 23, 1963.

photos.title: A note on the obituary
photos.caption: The obituary ran in the Becker County Record on Thursday, September 10, 1970, page 5. There are a few errors. Gerhard actually arrived in northern Minnesota in 1948; his failed tenure at Tenstrike and Blackduck is ignored. His brother was Ernest, not Earnest; his sister was Frieda, not Freda; his sister Adelheid was Mrs. Hilbert Nickel, not Mrs. Henry; and his sister Norma, oddly, was not mentioned.

name: Thomas Crawley Knowles   Volume III
summary: Starting his third year with the prospect of real school ‚Äì the Richmond Waldorf School.

photos.title: Beach Boy
photos.caption: Thomas capped a day at the beach with the first ice cream cone of the day. Susan, George, James, and Thomas were spending a week on the Virginia shore in mid-June 2019.

photos.title: Now we are three
photos.caption: Thomas and his guests had a backyard petting zoo at his third birthday: pony (saddled for rides), pig, goat, bunnies and more.

photos.title: And cake too!
photos.caption: Thomas and his birthday party guests had a backyard full of petting zoo farm animals, but everyone moved inside for the big event.

photos.title: Not quite a steering wheel
photos.caption: Thomas was exploring a few passenger and freight cars at the Science Museum’s collection of real railroad equipment.

photos.title: Swinger
photos.caption: A bedsheet hammock strung underneath the dining room table was an inventive way to pass some of the time during the pandemic stay-at-home.

photos.title: On Golden Pond
photos.caption: Thomas and his family spent a few days at the waterfront getaway of some family friends. Lots to explore: crabs, minnows, turtles, jellyfish and salt water.

photos.title: Schoolkid
photos.caption: Thomas, 5, in his first year of “real” school. He loves it.

name: The Game of Five Rocks
summary: A long-standing outdoors tradition of the Portland Leroi-Nickel household asks hikers to gather five rocks of interest and then create a cairn-like five-rock assemblage ‚Äî sometimes for evaluation, explication or discussion before an impromptu panel of judges. This is the December 2020 edition.

photos.title: Five Rocks (a)
photos.caption: A chunk of igneous rock dominates everything, yet the smallest smooth stone finds a natural spotlight.

photos.title: Five Rocks (b)
photos.caption: Each smooth rock offers a window into complex interior. All windows face forward.

photos.title: Five Rocks (c)
photos.caption: One rock commands the group, suggesting orator to audience, conductor to choir, teacher to class.

photos.title: Five Rocks (d)
photos.caption: A cruciform shape suggestive of a human figure relies on a sturdy, though pinpoint, rock tip for balance.

photos.title: Five Rocks (e)
photos.caption: Harmony and contrast. Two similar dark-gray egg-shaped rocks, worn smooth by water, join three rough-edged chunks of different dimensions.

name: Leia Reva Nickel 1
summary: Leia Reva Nickel arrived in Brooklyn on Thursday, December 10, 2020.

photos.title: Newborn
photos.caption: Leia Reva Nickel arrived in Brooklyn on Thursday, December 10, 2020. She checked in at 7 pounds, 11 ounces, and 20.5 inches.

photos.title: Perfect place for a nap
photos.caption: Leia slipped right into the family routine with Mom, Dad, and Big Brother Archie.

photos.title: Big yawn
photos.caption: At home on Mom’s lap, comfy, fed, and not a care in the world.

photos.title: A month already
photos.caption: Leia just about a month old: Alert, eyes tracking movement, happy.

photos.title: Thursday’s child
photos.caption: Thursday, January 21 – about five weeks old.

photos.title: Head up, eyes looking ahead
photos.caption: Leia at six weeks.

photos.title: Beauty and the beets
photos.caption: Archie yanked the beets out of the garden soil. Anson and Archie washed them with garden spritzer (on a hot day). Opa cooked them. Leia ate them by the handful and enjoyed herself immensely.

photos.title: Guess who?
photos.caption: There's a lot to see and do in a mirror. Leia preferred to look straight at the camera ... or its reflection.

photos.title: Generations
photos.caption: Oma and Leia had lots of time to get acquainted sitting on the “plop couch,” a favorite piece of family room furniture.

photos.title: Peace defined
photos.caption: Leia’s great-great-uncle Gerhard once remarked that sleeping infants “are in cahoots with the angels.”

photos.title: Fit for a queen
photos.caption: Leia tried out the child’s chair great-uncle Jim made in 1989 for his niece Ashley, now in her 30s. The maquette-scale piece is styled after *Three Chairs for Queens College,* a large sculptural work installed on the Queens College campus in October 1984.

photos.title: Mistress of Bisque
photos.caption: At 14 months, Leia uses her spoon to get drippy tomato soup from the bowl to her mouth. A healthy portion made the trip successfully.

photos.title: Maestra
photos.caption: Leia discovered her great-grandmother’s Steinway on a visit to Opa and Oma’s house in late May. She seemed to like the bass notes best.

name: Archie and Leia
summary: About two and a half years apart, Archie and Leia are growing up across from Prospect Park in Brooklyn.

photos.title: And then there were four
photos.caption: With the new, larger family settled in at 101 Prospect Park SW, Archie began reading books to his 9-day-old sister.

photos.title: Bears and wubs and toys – Oh my!
photos.caption: Big Brother Archie brought out some of his most prized possessions — especially bears and wubs — for Little Sister Leia to explore.

photos.title: Big Brother, Kid Sister
photos.caption: Leia is just about two months old.

photos.title: Cartographer
photos.caption: Leia appears to be lending a hand during a family hunt for Honeycrisps, Galas, and Fujis in a New Jersey orchard. They picked plenty — enough for a homemade apple tart.

name: Heirlooms
summary: It‚Äôs difficult to know in advance which items from the family‚Äôs physical record will attain heirloom status. The passage of time makes those choices. Here are a few.

photos.caption: After he sold Bosenbüttel, Fritz Mueller moved most of his money to America through Bremen banks. He kept some of it in gold coins, which he put into this money belt that Johanne wore on the emigration to Indiana.

photos.title: Georg Wilhelm Mueller
photos.caption: A charcoal sketch, possibly created by his son Fritz Mueller, appeared on a Family Search website in 2018. A note on the back says “by FGW Mueller” and has the date as 1865. It may have been handed down by Gertrude Mueller Rehwinkel.

photos.title: Heirloom
photos.caption: Georg Mueller owned this tobacco box late in the 18th century. He gave it to his son Fritz, who took it along when he and his family emigrated to Kendallville, Indiana, in 1865.

photos.title: The original
photos.caption: When the company’s first order for motors was canceled part way through production, Mal designed this fan and McMillan Manufacturing sold several thousand copies of it to keep the company going.

name: Nickels in Milwaukee
summary: Aunt Hattie was the only Nickel to go to high school. Hilbert, Al and Megs‚Äô only child, made it through Marquette University, married, and moved to Chicago.

photos.title: *Verheiratung*
photos.caption: The archives do not have the wedding date, but Al and Megs celebrated their fiftieth anniversary in the late 1950s.

photos.title: On South Howell
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel, maybe two or three years old, did not seem particularly pleased to sit for a family portrait.

photos.title: Confirmand
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel was confirmed at St. Lucas Lutheran Church, probably 1919 or 1920.

photos.title: The *Groß* American Game
photos.caption: **The *Groß* American Game:** Al Nickel (standing, fourth from right) and his baseball team probably played in Humboldt Park. His son Hilbert described how Al would knock out towering flies for kids there.

photos.title: High school
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel’s high school graduation picture.

photos.title: College grad
photos.caption: H.C. Nickel, graduating from Marquette, was the first Nickel to attend college.

photos.title: Immigrants
photos.caption: Al Nickel and Margaret Ziesemer were born in Germany, emigrated before their first birthdays, met, married, and lived in their own house on South Howell Avenue in Milwaukee.

photos.title: Nickels on Howell
photos.caption: Al and Megs owned a two-flat at 2617 South Howell Avenue and lived on the top floor. Al’s two sisters, Emma (Maas) and Hattie, lived two doors north at 2609, which the Maas family rented. The archive has no information about William, the fourth sibling, except that he was an unkind domestic partner.
